frozen_components: LLM residual stream activations used as SAE input
architecture.summary: The paper develops a methodology to compare Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) feature spaces across different large language models. SAEs are trained on frozen LLM residual stream activations to disentangle polysemantic neurons into monosemantic feature representations. The system pairs features across models via activation correlation, then measures spatial similarity of paired features using rotation-invariant representational similarity measures (SVCCA and RSA) applied to SAE decoder weight matrices. Information flows from LLM activations → SAE training → feature pairing → geometric similarity scoring.
trained_components: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) with learned encoder and decoder weights

biblio.title: Quantifying Feature Space Universality Across Large Language Models via Sparse Autoencoders

claims.statement: Even if SAEs across different models learn different feature representations, the spaces spanned by SAE features are similar, such that one SAE space is similar to another SAE space under rotation-invariant transformations (Analogous Feature Universality hypothesis).

claims.dataset: OpenWebText (100 samples, max sequence length 300, 30k tokens)
claims.statement: For almost all layer pairs after layer 0 in Pythia-70m vs Pythia-160m, SVCCA p-values are between 0.00 to 0.01, indicating statistically meaningful feature space similarities.

claims.dataset: OpenWebText (150 samples, max sequence length 150, 22.5k tokens)
claims.statement: For Gemma-1-2B vs Gemma-2-2B, almost all layer pair p-values for both SVCCA and RSA are between 0% to 1%, indicating statistically meaningful feature space similarities.

claims.dataset: OpenWebText (150 samples, max sequence length 150, 22.5k tokens)
claims.statement: For Gemma-2-2B vs Gemma-2-9B layer pair L11 vs L21, SVCCA score is 0.70 with mean random pairing score of 0.009.

claims.dataset: OpenWebText (100 samples, max sequence length 100, 10k tokens)
claims.statement: For Llama 3-8B-Instruct vs Llama 3.1-8B at Layer 25, SVCCA score is 0.3 and mean activation correlation is 66% after filtering to 7% of Llama 3.1-8B feature pairs.

Middle layers have the highest SVCCA and RSA scores; early layers have lower scores due to lacking content, while later layers have lower scores than middle layers, possibly due to being too specific/complex.

Semantic feature subspaces (e.g., 'Emotions', 'Time', 'Calendar') exhibit notably high similarity across models and pass statistical significance tests, while approximately 10-30% (typically 20%) of feature pairs are retained after filtering non-concept and many-to-1 pairings.

A notable fraction of features are idiosyncratic to different SAEs and not captured universally; approximately 70-90% of initial feature pairings are filtered out, indicating that not all SAE features have corresponding analogues across models.
